title: Binary Substituition

content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS. He can replace any occurrence of -


010101 with aaa
101010 with bbb
010010010 with ababab
101101101 with bababa
While doing these operations, Chef noticed that he can end up with different strings depending upon the order of application of the operations. Given the final string containing only aaa and bbb, Chef wants to know the number of possible strings he might have began with.

As the number of initial strings can be large, print the result modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input denoting SSS, the final string obtained after applying the operations.


Output Format
For each test case, output the number of original strings that can result in the final string mod 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: Sum of |S| over all test cases does not exceed 500000
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting only of characters 'a' and 'b', representing the final string after all substitutions.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of possible original binary strings that can produce S, modulo 998244353.

solution.approach:
Each character of the final string can be taken as a block of length 1. Additionally, any two consecutive characters that are different can be merged into a block of length 2 (corresponding to the patterns "ab" or "ba" in the final string). Therefore the problem reduces to counting the number of ways to partition the string into blocks of size 1 or 2 where a size‑2 block must consist of two different characters. Let dp[i] be the number of ways to partition the prefix of length i. The recurrence is:
- dp[0] = 1 (empty prefix)
- dp[1] = 1 (only one block of size 1)
- For i ≥ 2:
    * If S[i‑2] == S[i‑1] then the i‑th character cannot be paired with the previous one, so dp[i] = dp[i‑1].
    * Otherwise it can either stand alone or be paired with the previous character, giving dp[i] = dp[i‑1] + dp[i‑2].
All operations are performed modulo 998244353. The DP runs in O(|S|) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|S|) per test case, O(total length) overall.

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # i is the test case index (0 <= i <= 20).
    # The function should print the complete input for the i-th hidden test case.
    # Below is a simple deterministic generator matching the hidden cases defined above.
    cases = [
        "1
a",
        "1
b",
        "1
aa",
        "1
bb",
        "1
abababababababa",
        "1
aaaaaaaaaaaaaaa",
        "1
bbbbbbbbbbbbbbb",
        "1
aabbaaabbaaabbb",
        "1
aaaaa",
        "1
bbbbb",
        "1
ababab",
        "1
bababa",
        "1
abbaabbaabbaab",
        "1
aaabbbababaab",
        "1
ababaabbabba",
        "1
ababababab",
        "3
a
ab
aba",
        "4
b
ba
bab
baba",
        "5
aa
bb
ab
ba
abab",
        "2
abababababababa
bbbbbbbbbbbbbbb"
    ]
    print(cases[i])
